coredata.dc:title: Determination of Fe(Ⅲ) ion and cellular bioimaging based on a novel photoluminescent silicon nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Ye, Hong-Li
Shang, Yue
Wang, Hai-Yan
Ma, Yan-Li
He, Xi-Wen
Li, Wen-You
Li, Yu-Hao
Zhang, Yu-Kui
coredata.dc:description: The determination approaches of Fe (Ⅲ) in biological samples were developed by a novel water-soluble silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs). The SiNPs were synthesized by a facile microwave-assisted method, and simultaneously featured strong blue fluorescence (photoluminescence quantum yield: 25.2%), long lifetime (~13.29 ns) and good photo-stability. The fluorescence intensities of SiNPs were gradually quenched with Fe (Ⅲ) concentration increasing from 2.0 to 50 μmol/L. The detection limit of the established method was 0.56 μmol/L and the precision for eleven replicate detections of 20 μmol/L Fe (Ⅲ) was 3.2% (relative standard deviation, RSD). The spiked recoveries were 99.0%–104.5%. Results of the lifetime decay and cyclic voltammetry (CV) evidenced that the electron transfer was responsible for the fluorescence quenching mechanism of SiNPs and Fe (Ⅲ). Moreover, the SiNPs were successfully applied in the determination of Fe(Ⅲ) in different environmental waters and human serum. Finally, the resulting SiNPs exhibited the green fluorescence in HeLa cells as the optical probe.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Determination
Fe(Ⅲ) ion
Fluorescence
Silicon nanoparticles
Electron transfer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of metal ions with SiNPs
In vitro bioimaging of the as-prepared SiNPs
Detection of Fe(Ⅲ) ion with the as-prepared SiNPs
Ion selectivity tests with the as-prepared SiNPs
Discrimination of Fe (Ⅲ) with Fe (Ⅱ) based on the as-prepared SiNPs
Fluorescence quenching mechanism between Fe(Ⅲ) ion and the as-prepared SiNPs
Application of the as-prepared SiNPs in real samples
In vitro cytotoxicity assay of SiNPs
Application of SiNPs in vitro fluorescence imaging

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (nos. 21775077 and 81671179 ) and the Tianjin Natural Science Foundation (nos. 16JCZDJC37200 and 15JCYBJC24400 ). The authors thank Associate researcher Cong Kong of East China Sea Fisheries Research Institute, Chinese Academy of Fishery Sciences for the measurement of cyclic voltammetry curve.

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (nos. 21775077 and 81671179) and the Tianjin Natural Science Foundation (nos. 16JCZDJC37200 and 15JCYBJC24400). The authors thank Associate researcher Cong Kong of East China Sea Fisheries Research Institute, Chinese Academy of Fishery Sciences for the measurement of cyclic voltammetry curve.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the as-prepared SiNPs; (b) Size distribution histogram of the as-prepared SiNPs; (c) EDS image of the as-prepared SiNPs; (d) FTIR spectrum of the as-prepared SiNPs.
Survey XPS spectrum (a) and high resolution XPS spectra (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, (d) O 1s, and (e) Si 2p peaks of SiNPs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum (UV), fluorescence excitation spectrum (PEL) and fluorescence emission spectrum (PL) of the as-prepared SiNPs. Inset: the as-prepared SiNPs aqueous solution under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV lamp (right); (b) The excitation wavelength-independent emission behavior of the as-prepared; (c) The up-conversion fluorescence of the as-prepared SiNPs; (d) Photo-stability of SiNPs and FITC dye.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the as-prepared SiNPs response to various ions with the measurement parameters of excitation slit of 10 nm, emission slit of 5 nm, and voltage of 700 V; (b) Homogenization intensities of the as-prepared SiNPs response to various ions. The results were assessed by three separate parallel replicates.

(a) The fluorescence spectra of the as-prepared SiNPs exposure to different amounts of Fe (Ⅲ) from 0, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200, 500 μmol/L to 1, 2, 5, 8 mmol/L, respectively. Set the excitation slit to 10 nm, the emission slit to 5 nm, and the voltage of 700 V; (b) The linear relationship between (F0/F-1) and the concentration of Fe (Ⅲ) ranging from 2, 5, 10, 20, to 30, 40, 50 μmol/L.
Response between the as-prepared SiNPs, EDTA and Fe (Ⅲ). (a) The fluorescence spectra; (b) The lifetime decay curves.

Response between the as-prepared SiNPs and Fe (Ⅲ) (a, b) or Fe (Ⅱ) (c, d). (a) The fluorescence spectra of SiNPs with the concentration of Fe (Ⅲ) varied from 0 to 2 mmol/L; (b) The lifetime decay curves of SiNPs with the concentration of Fe (Ⅲ) varied from 0 to 2 mmol/L; (c) The fluorescence spectra of SiNPs with the concentration of Fe (Ⅱ) varied from 0 to 2 mmol/L; (d) The lifetime decay curves of SiNPs with the concentration of Fe (Ⅱ) varied from 0 to 2 mmol/L.
Response between the as-prepared SiNPs, H2O2 and Fe (Ⅱ). (a) The fluorescence spectra; (b) The lifetime decay curves.

(a) The cyclic voltammetry curve of SiNPs; (b) Band gap calculated from the UV absorption spectrum of SiNPs. A, h and ν represent the absorbance, the Planck constant and the frequency of radiating electromagnetic waves.

Viability of HeLa cells after incubation for 12 h, 24 h and 48 h with different concentrations (0, 50, 100, 150, 200 μg/mL) of the as-prepared SiNPs. All results were valuated with the mean ± standard deviation by three separate parallel replicates.
The confocal images of HeLa cells in the bright field and in the fluorescence field after being incubated with 20 μg/mL SiNPs for 8 h.
The synthesis schematic diagram of SiNPs.
The possible electron transfer process from SiNPs to Fe (Ⅲ).
Comparison of the fluoresecnt probes for the determination of Fe (Ⅲ).
The determination of Fe (Ⅲ) concentration in environmental water samples and human serum based on the fluorescent SiNPs.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Plus or minus five parallel determination average standard deviation.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b

head.ce:title: Determination of Fe(Ⅲ) ion and cellular bioimaging based on a novel photoluminescent silicon nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Research Center for Analytical Sciences, State Key Laboratory of Medicinal Chemical Biology, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Biosensing and Molecular Recognition, Nankai University, Tianjin, 300071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Medicinal Chemical Biology
Tianjin Key Laboratory of Biosensing and Molecular Recognition

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Chromatographic Research and Analysis Center, Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Dalian, 116023, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Chromatographic Research and Analysis Center

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Chromatographic Research and Analysis Center, Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Dalian 116023, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The determination approaches of Fe (Ⅲ) in biological samples were developed by a novel water-soluble silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs). The SiNPs were synthesized by a facile microwave-assisted method, and simultaneously featured strong blue fluorescence (photoluminescence quantum yield: 25.2%), long lifetime (~13.29 ns) and good photo-stability. The fluorescence intensities of SiNPs were gradually quenched with Fe (Ⅲ) concentration increasing from 2.0 to 50 μmol/L. The detection limit of the established method was 0.56 μmol/L and the precision for eleven replicate detections of 20 μmol/L Fe (Ⅲ) was 3.2% (relative standard deviation, RSD). The spiked recoveries were 99.0%–104.5%. Results of the lifetime decay and cyclic voltammetry (CV) evidenced that the electron transfer was responsible for the fluorescence quenching mechanism of SiNPs and Fe (Ⅲ). Moreover, the SiNPs were successfully applied in the determination of Fe(Ⅲ) in different environmental waters and human serum. Finally, the resulting SiNPs exhibited the green fluorescence in HeLa cells as the optical probe.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the essential trace elements, iron is an important part of sulfur protein and heme iron. Many physiological processes require the participation of iron ion, such as oxygen transport and metabolism, regulation and control of gene transcription, electron transfer process [1–4]. Iron deficiency or excess will cause liver disease, anemia, alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's and so on [5–9]. Therefore, it is practical significance for environment and human health to study the iron content. At present, many methods for the determination of iron ion have been developed by atomic absorption spectrometry [10], electrochemical method [11–13], colorimetric method [14], mass spectrometry [15] and so on. Among various strategies, fluorescence method has drawn considerable attention for their following superiorities, such as low cost, easy operation, high sensitivity and good selectivity [16–20]. The common fluorescence probes including organic fluorescent dyes [21–23], quantum dots [24,25], gold and silver nanoclusters [26,27] and carbon dots [28,29], have been applied to detect Fe (Ⅲ). However, the synthetic processes of organic fluorescent dyes are complex and they have poor resistance to light bleaching. Quantum dots contain toxic ingredients, e.g. Cd element. And the synthetic cost of gold and silver nanoclusters is high. So it is necessary to design a novel probe for detection of Fe (Ⅲ) with easy operation, low cost, good stability and environmentally friendly.

Silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs) as a novel biological fluorescent probe have been received widespread attention owing to their outstanding features of low cost, simple preparation, controllable surface, excellent optical properties, strong resistance to photobleaching, low biological toxicity etc [30–32]. SiNPs have been applied in detections of Cu [33], Hg [34], dopamine [35], heparin [36], 2, 4, 6-trinitrophenol [37] and so on. However, using SiNPs as the probe in the determination of Fe (Ⅲ) has not been reported.

Herein, we develop the determination approach of Fe (Ⅲ) based on a novel photoluminescent SiNPs, which were synthesized by one-pot microwave-assisted method, and simultaneously featured bright blue fluorescence (photoluminescence quantum yield: 25.2%), long lifetime (~13.29 ns), superbly strong photo-stability, favorable resistance to interference. The probe was utilized to detect Fe (Ⅲ), and the fluorescence quenching mechanism was evidenced by lifetime decay and cyclic voltammetry. The established method featured the merits of wide response range of 2.0~50 μmol/L and a lower limit detection of 0.56 μmol/L. The SiNPs was successfully applied in Fe (Ⅲ) detection in water samples and human serum. Finally, the resulting SiNPs exhibited the green fluorescence in HeLa cells as the optical probe.

Glycerol, disodium hydrogen phosphate dodecahydrate (Na2HPO4·12H2O), N-[3-(trimethoxysilyl)propyl]ethylenesiamine (DAMO, ≥ 99.5%), sodium dihydrogen phosphate dihydrate (NaH2PO4·2H2O), fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC), Rhodamine 6G (Rh6G) and trisodium citrate dehydrate (SC, ≥ 99.0%) were from J&K Scientific Ltd. (Beijing, China) and used without additional purification. Ultrapure water was utilized throughout the experiments. Human cervical carcinoma cell lines (HeLa) were supplied by Nankai University School of Medicine. Thiazolyl blue tetrazolium bromide (MTT) was purchased from Keygen Biotech (Jiangsu, China).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image were recorded on a Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscope (FEI, The Netherlands). Microwave reactor was purchased from Xi'an Yu Hui instrument Co., Ltd (Shaanxi, China). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was performed by a Vector 22 FTIR spectrophotometer (Bruker, Germany) with a range of 4000–400 cm−1. UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) was employed to depict the UV–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra, and F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) for recording the photoluminescence (PL) spectra. If not specified, the fluorescence measurement parameters were setting the excitation slit to 5 nm, the emission slit to 5 nm, and the voltage to 700 V. The absolute quantum yield values and the photoluminescence lifetime decay curves were measured by an FLS-920 spectrophotometer (Edinburgh, UK). Cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves were performed by CHI 1242C potentiostat (Shanghai, China) with a standard three-electrode system and with the scan rate of 0.1 mV/s at room temperature. Gold electrode, saturated calomel electrode and platinum electrode were utilized as the working electrode, reference electrode and counter electrode, respectively. The electrolyte solution was 0.1 mol/L PBS buffer solution (pH = 7.0) with 0.1 mg/mL SiNPs. The imaging photographs were captured by FV 1000 confocal microscope (Olympus, Japan) under 405 nm excitation with the emission window of 500–600 nm. All optical measurements were utilized at room temperature.

The preparation of fluorescence SiNPs were similar to the literature reported previously [38]. Briefly, 0.3180 g citrate was dispersed into 8 mL Ar-saturated glycerol solution and vigorously stirred for 20 min. Then, 3 mL N-[3-(trimethoxysilyl)propyl]ethylenesiamine as the silicon source was added into the homogeneous reaction system dropwise. The mixture was stirred for another 10 min. The transparent precursor solution was transferred into the microwave reactor under normal pressure, then reacted at 180 °C for 15 min. The dark brown solution suggested the generation of SiNPs. The excess reagents were removed by dialysis (MWCO: 1000 Da). The purified product was freeze-dried and dissolved into ultrapure water for further characterizations and applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of metal ions with SiNPs
ce:section.ce:para: The stock solutions of Zn2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Na+, K+, Mn2+, Co2+, Fe3+ and Fe2+were prepared by dissolving their chloride or perchlorate salts in deionized water. A series of solutions of fresh SiNPs and metal ions were added into the centrifugal tubes. The deionized water was utilized to fix the final volume to 2 mL. The ultimate density of metal ions was 2 mmol/L. The mixed solutions were dramatically stirred for 10 s and then waiting for about 15 min to carry out the fluorescence measurements with the excitation wavelength of 385 nm and the emission wavelength of 480 nm. The fluorescence intensity was recorded and used to estimate the quenching effects. Each sample was performed three separate parallel replicates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro bioimaging of the as-prepared SiNPs
ce:section.ce:para: The HeLa cells were cultured with 5% CO2 and 500 μL complete DMEM at 37 °C for 24 h. Then, the cells were exposed to SiNPs at 20 μg/mL and incubated for 8 h. Cells were then washed with 0.1 mol/L phosphate buffer saline (PBS, pH 7.2) for 1 h, fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde (PFA) in the dark for 15 min, and sealed on the glass slides with mounting medium. Cells treated with free-SiNPs were used as the control under the same operation.
Other experimental details including the measurement of photoluminescence quantum yield and cytotoxicity assay were depicted in Supplementary Materials.

Scheme 1 shows the preparation of SiNPs, which were synthesized by one-pot microwave-assisted synthesis method at atmospheric pressure, taking 3-(2-aminoethylamino)-propyltrimethoxysilane as silicon source and sodium citrate as the reducing agent. Zhong et al. reported the advantages of microwave-assisted synthesis method such as the high reaction rate, the uniform product formation for the homogeneous heating and the high reaction selectivity due to diﬀerent dipole constants of various substances, which are beneficial to the rapid preparation of uniform size materials [39].

Fig. 1 displays the structural characterization of SiNPs. As observed in Fig. 1a, SiNPs appeared spherical particles with good and uniform monodispersity. The average size of SiNPs was 1.45 ± 0.01 nm, which was counted by more than 225 particles (Fig. 1b). The small size particles (<10 nm) feature less potential toxicity for their quicker and easier renal clearance in vivo than the large ones [40]. In addition, the EDX pattern revealed that SiNPs contained C, N, O, Si and Cu, respectively. It should be particularly pointed out that the Cu element and most of C element stemmed from the carbon-coated copper grids used for the EDX measurement (Fig. 1c). FTIR spectrum evidenced the successful synthesis of SiNPs, as described in Fig. 1d. The absorption peaks at 1062 cm−1 and 2945 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of Si–O and the antisymmetric stretching vibration of C–H, respectively. And the strong absorption peaks of 1615 cm−1 and 3418 cm−1 were respectively assigned to the stretching vibration and the bending vibration of N–H.

XPS spectra confirmed the surface composition of SiNPs, as shown in Fig. 2 . The survey XPS spectrum (Fig. 2a) verified the surface of SiNPs contained the C, N, O, Si elements, which was consistent with the results of EDS. The C 1s spectrum (Fig. 2b) revealed the three peaks of 285.1, 286.4 and 288.0 eV, which were attributed to C–C/C–Si, C–O and CO, respectively [41]. The peaks in N1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) at 399.9 and 400.5 eV were respectively ascribed to N–C and N–H bond [42]. The original O 1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) was fitted to the three signals of 531.0, 532.2, and 533.7 eV, which were assigned to CO, C–O and Si–O, respectively [43]. Fig. 2e evidences the presence of Si–C (102.5 eV) and Si–O (103.4 eV) in Si 2p spectrum [44]. Results of the high resolution XPS spectra were in agreement with that of FTIR.

Fig. 3 shows the optical characterization of SiNPs. Fig. 3a describes UV–vis absorption spectrum, fluorescence excitation spectrum and fluorescence emission spectrum. As shown in Fig. 3a, the SiNPs had an obvious absorption at 350 nm, an excitation peak at 385 nm and an emission peak at 488 nm. All optical spectra appeared high symmetry. The inset in Fig. 3a displays that SiNPs solution appeared transparent under daylight and emitted bright blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation. The emission wavelength of the as-prepared SiNPs was almost unchanged (Fig. 3b) with the excitation wavelength ranging from 300 to 430 nm at equal intervals, which suggested that the as-synthesized SiNPs displayed the wavelength-independent emission behavior. The result was consistent with the reported literatures [45–47]. Furthermore, the SiNPs exhibited obvious up-conversion fluorescence, which was shown in Fig. 3c. The emission wavelength located at 488 nm with the excitation wavelength increasing from 600 to 880 nm. This property might stem from the two-photon absorption process [48,49]. Likewise, compared with the traditional organic fluorescent dye FITC, SiNPs demonstrated strong optical stability. As displayed in Fig. 3d, the fluorescence intensity of FITC remained 24% of the initial intensity after 15 min irradiation, while that of the SiNPs kept 98% after 180 min irradiation, which indicated the strong fluorescence stability of the as-prepared SiNPs.

Calculated by Eq. S(1) and Eq. S(2), the relative photoluminescence quantum yield (RPLQY) and the absolute photoluminescence quantum yield (APLQY) of SiNPs were 20.7% and 25.2%, respectively (Figs. S1a and S1b), which suggested the strong fluorescence of SiNPs. The long fluorescence lifetime demonstrates the excellent optical properties of the fluorescent materials. As showed in Fig. S1c, the fluorescence decay curve of the SiNPs presented a slower fluorescence decay than those of the traditional organic fluorescent dyes R6G and FITC. According to the fitting results listed in Table S1, the fluorescence decay of SiNPs matched well with quadratic exponential decay model (χ2 = 1.026), and the fitting values were 3.27 ns (8.37%) and 14.20 ns (91.63%), of which the short lifetime value might be caused by surface defects or surface trap rapid fluorescence radiation [50,51]. The lifetimes of FITC, R6G and SiNPs were 3.83 ns, 4.13 ns and 13.29 ns, respectively. Long fluorescent life is much easier to distinguish it from the auto-fluorescence (τ < 5 ns) of the organisms and achieve long-range fluorescence imaging [52]. Fig. S1d shows the pH stability in water and PBS of SiNPs. The fluorescence intensities of SiNPs were almost impervious to the pH varied from 2 to 11 in water and 4.5 to 9 in PBS, which exhibited the satisfactory pH stability of SiNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe(Ⅲ) ion with the as-prepared SiNPs
Ion selectivity tests with the as-prepared SiNPs
ce:section.ce:para: 12 kinds of common metal ions, Zn2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Na+, K+, Mn2+, Co2+, Fe3+ and Fe2+ were utilized to investigate the selectivity of SiNPs to metal ions. The fluorescence intensities of SiNPs were almost unchanged in the presence of the Zn2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Na+, K+, Mn2+, Co2+ and Fe2+ ions, respectively (Fig. 4 a). However, the fluorescence intensity of SiNPs decreased sharply in the presence of Fe (Ⅲ) with the same concentration as the other ions. Fig. 4b clearly demonstrates the quenching effect of Fe (Ⅲ) on the SiNPs with the error bars.

In order to get the best detection effect, the reaction conditions were optimized, including the reaction time (Fig. S2a), the reaction solvent (Fig. S2b), the pH (PBS) (Fig. S2c), and the anions of iron-compound (Fig. S2d). As shown in Fig. S2a, the fluorescence intensities achieved the platform as the reaction time increased to 10 min. Therefore, we detected the fluorescence intensities of the reaction system after the reaction carried out 10 min. Fig. S2b and Fig. S2c show the influences of the solvent and pH on the detection, respectively. And the results indicated that both H2O and PBS were the favorable solvent for the detection reaction. In view of the pH effect on the reaction system, PBS (pH 7.00) was used into the detection test. Anions of the iron-compounds had no influence on the reaction (Fig. S2d), and all of FeCl3, Fe2(SO4)3 and Fe(NO3)3 could be adopted into the detection test.

The fluorescence intensities of SiNPs decreased with Fe (Ⅲ) concentration varied from 0 to 8 mmol/L under the optimal experimental conditions (Fig. 5 a), and the fluorescence quenching of SiNPs had a linear relation, which conformed to the Stern-Volmer equation Eq (1):
Eq (1)
F 0
/
F
=
K sv
[ Q ]
+
1
where F0 was the fluorescence intensity of SiNPs in the absence of Fe (Ⅲ) ion and F was that in the presence of Fe (Ⅲ) ion. Ksv and Q represent the Stern-Volmer quenching constant and the Fe (Ⅲ)ion concentration, respectively. As obviously displayed in Fig. 5b, the fluorescence intensities of SiNPs decreased gradually with the concentration of Fe (Ⅲ) increasing from 2.0 to 50 μmol/L. The relationship between the (F0/F-1) and the Fe (Ⅲ) concentration showed a good linearity (R2 = 0.9974) with a detection limit of 0.56 μmol/L (S/N = 3) and RSD (relative standard deviation) of 3.2% for 11 replicate measurements of Fe (Ⅲ) at 20 μmol/L. The Ksv value for SiNPs was calculated as 3.84 × 103 M−1, which was nearly consistent with the reported value of 2.3 × 103 M−1 [53]. Table 1 summarizes the reported detection method by other fluorescence nanoparticles, which showed that the established method in our work was comparable to the literatures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discrimination of Fe (Ⅲ) with Fe (Ⅱ) based on the as-prepared SiNPs
ce:section.ce:para: Fe (Ⅲ) possess the strong combining capacity with EDTA, while the fluorescence intensity and lifetime were not restored after adding moderate EDTA into the reaction system involving SiNPs and Fe (Ⅲ), which indicated that the Fe (Ⅲ) featured the strong ability to combine with SiNPs ( Figs. 6a and 6b).

Despite the strong quenching effect of Fe (Ⅲ) on SiNPs, Fe (Ⅱ) had almost no influence on the fluorescence intensity of SiNPs (Figs. S3a and S3b). As the concentration of Fe (Ⅲ) increased from 0.05 to 2 mmol/L, the fluorescence intensities and the lifetime decay of SiNPs decreased (Fig. 7 a and 7b). While the fluorescence intensities and lifetime decay of SiNPs were almost unchanged with the concentration of Fe (Ⅱ) increasing from 0.05 to 2 mmol/L (Fig. 7c and 7d). However, the fluorescence intensities dramatically decreased when the H2O2 was added into the reaction system contained SiNPs and Fe (Ⅱ), as obviously shown in Fig. 8 a. Meanwhile, the lifetime decay also dropped rapidly (Fig. 8b), which would be caused by the generation of Fe (Ⅲ) from Fe (Ⅱ) in the presence of H2O2. Consequently, SiNPs could be employed in discriminating Fe (Ⅲ) from Fe (Ⅱ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching mechanism between Fe(Ⅲ) ion and the as-prepared SiNPs
ce:section.ce:para: The possible quenching mechanism between Fe (Ⅲ) ion and SiNPs was investigated. The fluorescence quenching may be resulted from the interaction between Fe (Ⅲ) and the hydroxy groups or amino groups of SiNPs [58]. Fig. S4 describes the UV absorption spectra of SiNPs with the increasing concentration of Fe (Ⅲ) from 0 to 50 μmol/L. It was observed that the UV absorption peak of SiNPs was no change with the increasing concentration of Fe (Ⅲ), which implied the presence of dynamic fluorescence quenching effect. At the same time, the fluorescence lifetime of SiNPs gradually decreased with the increase of Fe (Ⅲ) concentrations (Fig. 7b), which also indicated the dynamic fluorescence quenching effect contributed to the detection mechanism of SiNPs towards Fe (Ⅲ).

As shown in Fig. S5, the UV absorption spectrum of Fe (Ⅲ) (blue line) slightly overlapped with the UV absorption spectrum (green line) and the excitation spectrum (pink line) of SiNPs, which indicated that the fluorescence quenching mechanisms may be originated from photo-induced electron transfer (PET) and inner filter effect (IFE). Noticeably, the UV absorption spectrum of Fe (Ⅲ) (blue line) scarcely overlapped with the emission spectrum (orange line) of SiNPs, suggesting that the fluorescence quenching mechanism would not be from fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET).

To further verify whether photo-induced electron transfer quenched the fluorescence of SiNPs, the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) energy level of SiNPs were investigated by cyclic voltammetry (CV). As described in Fig. 9 a, the obvious reduction potential of SiNPs was measured to be −0.80 eV, which was in agreement with the reported literature [59]. ELUMO and EHOMO of SiNPs could be calculated by the following empirical formulas [60–62]:
Eq(2)
E LUMO
= - e
(
E red
+ 4.4 )
Eq(3)
E HOMO
= E
LUMO
- E
g
where Ered and Eg respectively represent the reduction potential and the band gap of SiNPs. The ELUMO of SiNPs was calculated to be −3.60 eV by Eq (2). Fig. 9b shows the band gap of SiNPs from UV absorption and Eg was obtained to be 3.88 eV. Then EHOMO of SiNPs was calculated to be −7.48 eV by Eq (3). The electrode potential of Fe (Ⅲ) was −4.36 eV [63]. Thus, the electron could transfer from LUMO of SiNPs to the half-filled d-shell of Fe (Ⅲ). Moreover, the crystal field stabilization energy (CFSE) between dγ and dε of Fe (Ⅲ) was determined to be 2.7 eV [28,64,65], which was less than the energy gap between LUMO and HOMO of SiNPs (i.e. 3.88 eV). Hence, the electron-transfer may occur among the LUMO of SiNPs, dγ of Fe (Ⅲ), dε of Fe (Ⅲ) and the HOMO of SiNPs, which may cause the fluorescence quenching of SiNPs. Scheme 2 shows the energy level of SiNPs and the possible electron transfer process from SiNPs to Fe (Ⅲ). In addition, IFE may also play a certain role in the fluorescence quenching mechanism of SiNPs for the detection of Fe (Ⅲ) [60,62].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the as-prepared SiNPs in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To further confirm the practicality of the as-prepared SiNPs in detecting Fe (Ⅲ) in real samples, the established method was applied to determine the Fe (Ⅲ) content in tap water, waste water and human serum (Table 2 ). The results (the first column in Table 2) were almost consistent with those measured by the ICP-AES method (the last column in Table 2), which verified the reliability of the proposed method. The concentration of Fe (Ⅲ) in human serum calculated by the proposed method was 80.47 mg/100 mL, which was almost identical to the reported value [66]. The standard addition method was adopted to verify the feasibility of the proposed method. The recoveries of these measurements were 104.5%, 103.6% and 99.0% for human serum, waste water, and tap water, respectively, which indicated the accuracy and reliability of the as-synthesized SiNPs for the determination of Fe (Ⅲ) in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity assay of SiNPs
ce:section.ce:para: In terms of its excellent fluorescence properties, SiNPs was further developed to be bioluminescence imaging probe in this work. First, we evaluated the cytotoxicity of SiNPs by MTT assay, and the results were shown in Fig. 10 . After the incubation for 12 h, 24 h and 48 h with the concentration of 0~200 μg/mL SiNPs, the survival rates of HeLa were still remained over 90% approximately, which indicated that SiNPs had low cytotoxicity in vitro.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of SiNPs in vitro fluorescence imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Featured the merits of controllable surface, excellent optical properties, strong stability and low biological toxicity, the resulted SiNPs was applied in fluorescence imaging in HeLa cells. As shown in Fig. 11 , compared with the control groups, there was no obvious change in the shape and morphology of HeLa cells after being incubated with 20 μg/mL SiNPs for 8 h in the bright field. On the contrary, HeLa cells exposing to SiNPs exhibited stronger green dotted fluorescent signals than those without SiNPs at the light of 405 nm excitation, which indicated that SiNPs were successfully entered into the cells and mainly located in the nuclei. Combined with the result of MTT assay, the SiNPs were available for bio-imaging in vitro.

In summary, we develop a facile and feasible method for detection of Fe (Ⅲ) based on the novel fluorescence probe of the water-soluble silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs). The fluorescence quenching of SiNPs was linearly dependent on the Fe (Ⅲ) concentration ranging from 2.0 to 50 μmol/L (R2 = 0.9974) with the limit of detection of 0.56 μmol/L and the RSD of 3.2% for eleven replicate detections of 20 μmol/L Fe (Ⅲ). A electron transfer fluorescence quenching mechanism between SiNPs and Fe (Ⅲ) was proposed and evidenced by lifetime decay and cyclic voltammetry. Moreover, the SiNPs were successfully applied to detect the Fe(Ⅲ) in different environmental waters and human serum. Finally, the resulting SiNPs exhibited the green fluorescence in HeLa cells as the optical probe.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (nos. 21775077 and 81671179) and the Tianjin Natural Science Foundation (nos. 16JCZDJC37200 and 15JCYBJC24400). The authors thank Associate researcher Cong Kong of East China Sea Fisheries Research Institute, Chinese Academy of Fishery Sciences for the measurement of cyclic voltammetry curve.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2021.122294.

Hong-Li Ye: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Yue Shang: Investigation, Data curation. Hai-Yan Wang: Investigation, Validation. Yan-Li Ma: Formal analysis, Data curation. Xi-Wen He: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Wen-You Li: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Yu-Hao Li: Funding acquisition, Supervision. Yu-Kui Zhang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.
